title: The Parameter vs. Compute Problem
concept: As models scale, running a forward pass through every parameter for every token becomes too expensive; we need a way to only activate relevant weights.

exercises.prompt: In AI, what does the term 'parameters' generally refer to?

options.text: The total weights and knowledge stored in the model.
The physical temperature of the server room.
The speed of the internet connection.

exercises.explain: Parameters are the weights and connections that hold the model's knowledge, similar to synapses in a brain.

cards.title: Super Smart, But Super Slow?
cards.body: As AI brains get bigger, they also get slower and require massive amounts of electricity. How can we make AI smarter without causing power grids to melt? The secret lies in changing how we use their brainpower.

cards.title: The Scaling Dilemma
cards.body: The 'Parameter vs. Compute' problem is the challenge of scaling up an AI's knowledge (parameters) without making it too slow or expensive to run (compute). If a model must activate all of its billions of parameters for every single word it generates, it quickly becomes too slow and costly for real-world use.

cards.title: The Library Analogy
cards.body: Imagine a massive library with millions of books. If you want to answer a question about cooking, you don't need to read every single book in the building. You only need to open the few cookbooks on the shelf. In the same way, we want our AI to only read the 'cookbooks' it needs for a specific prompt, instead of scanning the entire library every time.

cards.title: Dense vs. Sparse Processing
cards.body: A traditional Dense Model (like older GPTs) must wake up all of its 100 billion parameters to generate the word 'cat'. In contrast, a modern Sparse Model might have 100 billion parameters in total, but it only activates 10 billion of them (the ones related to animals and language) to generate the same word, saving 90% of the energy!

cards.title: The All-or-Nothing Myth
cards.body: You might think that a model with 100 billion parameters always requires 100 billion calculations per word. This is a myth! With smart routing techniques, a model can contain massive amounts of knowledge while only using a tiny fraction of its brainpower at any one moment.

cards.title: Dense vs. Sparse Routing
cards.body: See how different architectures handle the same word. Dense models brute-force the entire brain, while Sparse models target only what is needed.

left.text: Activates 100% of its parameters for every single token. High cost, slow speed.

right.text: Activates only a small subset of parameters per token. Low cost, high speed.

exercises.prompt: What happens if a model runs a forward pass through *every* parameter for *every* single word it processes?

options.text: It becomes extremely slow and expensive to run.
It becomes too small to remember anything.
It runs out of training data immediately.

exercises.explain: When every parameter is used for every single token, the compute cost scales directly with the model's total size, making large models incredibly expensive and slow.

exercises.prompt: True or False: We can reduce the computational cost of a model by only activating the relevant parts of its network for each word.
exercises.explain: This is true. By only activating a subset of parameters (e.g., in Mixture-of-Experts models), we can run massive models at a fraction of the cost.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the description of the scaling challenge:
exercises.sentence: When we increase parameters, we must find a way to keep the necessary ______ low so the AI remains fast.
exercises.explain: Compute refers to the actual processing power and calculations needed to run the model. If compute is too high, the AI becomes too slow to use.

exercises.prompt: Identify which AI system successfully solves the Parameter vs. Compute problem:

options.text: System A: 100B total parameters, 100B active per word.
System B: 100B total parameters, 10B active per word.
System C: 10B total parameters, 10B active per word.

exercises.explain: System B represents the goal of Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) or sparse models, which only use a small active portion of their total knowledge pool for any single word. System A is a traditional dense model, and System C is just a small model.

exercises.prompt: To solve the Parameter vs. Compute problem, what is the ideal balance we want to achieve?

options.text: Shrinking the overall size of the model so it fits on a smartphone.
Forcing the model to process all parameters twice as fast.
Keeping a massive pool of parameters but only activating a small fraction per token.
Removing all parameters and using a simple database instead.

exercises.explain: This is the core trade-off: keeping a massive brain (high parameters) to remember lots of facts, but only running a small portion of it (low active compute) so it remains fast and affordable to run.
